title: Probability Models
blurb: Sample spaces, events, equally-likely outcomes, complements, and the addition rule with Venn diagrams.
buildsOn: Counting (Units 1–3) — probability is counting favorable over counting all.

lessons.title: Probability Is Counting, Twice
lessons.concreteComparison: "What's the chance a random $2$-topping pizza from our $8$ toppings has mushrooms?" Act One counted the menu: $C(8,2) = 28$ pizzas total, and mushroom pizzas pair the mushroom with one of the $7$ others: $7$. Chance: $\frac{7}{28} = \frac{1}{4}$. Every probability in this act is two counts from Act One, stacked as a fraction.
lessons.objective: Compute probabilities of equally-likely outcomes as favorable-count over total-count, using the Act One toolkit for both counts.
concept: **Probability** lives on the scale $0$ (impossible) to $1$ (certain). For a sample space of EQUALLY LIKELY outcomes: $$P(\text{event}) = \frac{\text{favorable outcomes}}{\text{total outcomes}}$$
Both counts come from Act One — multiplication principle, permutations, combinations, complements. The mathematics is old; only the fraction is new.

The equally-likely requirement is real: outcomes must be the SAME kind of object, counted the same way. Mixing "pizzas" in the numerator with "toppings" in the denominator breaks the fraction — numerator and denominator must count the same universe.
Probabilities of all outcomes sum to $1$, so probabilities give you a free audit: an answer above $1$ or below $0$ isn't a probability, it's a counting bug wearing a costume.
lessons.keyIdea: P = favorable/total for equally likely outcomes — two Act One counts of the SAME universe, stacked.

facts.title: The definition
facts.explanation: Favorable outcomes over all outcomes, both counted in the same sample space S.

facts.title: The scale
facts.explanation: Impossible to certain; outside this range means the counting broke.

workedExamples.statement: A random $2$-topping pizza is built from $8$ toppings. What is the probability it includes mushrooms?
workedExamples.solution: Universe: $C(8,2) = 28$ pizzas. Favorable: mushroom + one of the other $7$ toppings $= 7$. $P = \frac{7}{28} = \frac{1}{4}$.

workedExamples.statement: Two dice are rolled. What is the probability the sum is $8$?
A $4$-digit PIN is chosen at random (digits $0$–$9$, repeats allowed). What is the probability all four digits are different?
workedExamples.solution: Universe: $10^4 = 10000$. Favorable: $10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7 = 5040$ (Unit 2's shrinking slots). $P = \frac{5040}{10000} = 0.504$ — a hair better than half.

commonMistakes.text: Counting the numerator and denominator in different universes.
commonMistakes.correction: If the denominator counts ORDERED rolls (36), the numerator must count ordered rolls too — (3,5) and (5,3) both. Choose one universe, count both layers in it.
Win-or-lose isn't 1/2 unless both are equally likely. The formula needs a sample space of EQUAL outcomes — build it from fair coins, fair dice, or uniform random draws, not from wishful symmetry.

tryIt.statement: A letter is drawn at random from БАЯРЛАЛАА ($9$ letters). What is the probability it is А?
A committee of $2$ is drawn at random from $4$ girls and $3$ boys. Probability both are girls?

steps.title: Two counts, one fraction
beats: "Chance a random 2-topping pizza has mushrooms?"
Total pizzas: $C(8,2) = 28$ — Act One.
Mushroom pizzas: $7$ — Act One again.
$P = \frac{7}{28} = \frac{1}{4}$. Probability is counting, stacked.

steps.title: The fraction's fine print
beats: Outcomes must be EQUALLY LIKELY — fair dice, fair draws.
Numerator and denominator count the SAME universe.
Scale: $0 \le P \le 1$; all outcomes together sum to $1$.
An answer of $1.4$ is a counting bug, not a probability.

steps.title: One die
steps.prompt: A fair die is rolled. $P(\text{multiple of } 3)$?
steps.explanation: Favorable: $\{3, 6\}$ — two of six: $\frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$. The $\frac{1}{6}$ counts only the $3$; $\frac{1}{2}$ overcounts.

steps.title: Ordered or not — just match
steps.prompt: Two coins flip. Bilguun says $P(\text{one head, one tail}) = \frac{1}{3}$ ("HH, one-of-each, TT — three outcomes"). What went wrong?
options: His three outcomes aren't equally likely
steps.explanation: "One of each" bundles TWO equally-likely outcomes (HT and TH), so his list isn't uniform. In the fair universe $\{HH, HT, TH, TT\}$: $P = \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$. Equal likelihood is a property you must CHECK, not assume.

steps.title: Name the universe out loud
steps.body: Start every problem with one sentence: "The universe is all ___ , and there are ___ of them." (All 2-topping pizzas: 28. All ordered dice rolls: 36.) Once the universe is named, the favorable count almost always follows from an Act One tool — and universe-mixing bugs become impossible.

steps.title: The 1654 problem that started it all
steps.body: Probability theory's founding question — the Chevalier de Méré's dice bets, settled by Pascal and Fermat — was answered exactly the way you just worked: count the equally-likely outcomes, count the favorable ones, divide. Three hundred and seventy years later, that's still lesson one, because it still works.

steps.title: What you learned
points: P(E) = favorable/total over equally likely outcomes.
Both counts use Act One tools, in ONE consistent universe.
0 ≤ P ≤ 1 and everything sums to 1 — free audits, always on.

lessons.title: Events & Complements
lessons.concreteComparison: An event isn't one outcome — it's a CLUB of outcomes. "The sum is even" admits $18$ of the $36$ dice rolls. And every club defines its non-members: "at least one six" has a complement — "no sixes" — whose members are easier to count: $\frac{25}{36}$ outside, so $\frac{11}{36}$ inside. You did this exact move with counts in Unit 1; now it wears probabilities.
lessons.objective: Treat events as sets of outcomes, and compute P(not A) = 1 − P(A), especially for at-least-one events.
concept: An **event** is a subset of the sample space — every outcome is in or out. Events can be described in words ("sum is even") but they ARE sets, and their probability is their size over the universe's size.
**The complement rule:** $A$ and not-$A$ split the universe, so $$P(\text{not } A) = 1 - P(A)$$ — Unit 1's total-minus-unwanted, divided through by the universe size.
"**At least one**" remains the trigger phrase: its complement "none" is a single clean count. $P(\text{at least one six in two dice}) = 1 - \frac{25}{36} = \frac{11}{36}$.

Special clubs: the **impossible event** (empty set, $P = 0$) and the **certain event** (whole space, $P = 1$). Every other event lives strictly between — and an event plus its complement always accounts for the entire $1$.
lessons.keyIdea: Events are outcome-sets; P(not A) = 1 − P(A), and 'at least one' flips to the single count 'none'.

facts.title: The complement rule
facts.explanation: The event and its complement partition the sample space, so their probabilities share the 1.

facts.title: At least one
facts.explanation: The sprawling event's complement is one clean multiplication.

workedExamples.statement: Two dice are rolled. Find $P(\text{at least one six})$.
workedExamples.solution: Complement — no sixes: each die has $5$ safe faces, $5 \times 5 = 25$ of $36$. $P(\text{none}) = \frac{25}{36}$, so $P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - \frac{25}{36} = \frac{11}{36}$.

workedExamples.statement: A family plans $4$ children. Assuming boy/girl equally likely, find $P(\text{at least one girl})$.
workedExamples.solution: Universe: $2^4 = 16$ birth orders. Complement — all boys: $1$ outcome. $P = 1 - \frac{1}{16} = \frac{15}{16}$.

workedExamples.statement: A $5$-card hand is dealt. Find $P(\text{at least one ace})$ as a formula and evaluate it as a fraction of hands.
workedExamples.solution: Complement — ace-free hands: $C(48,5) = 1{,}712{,}304$ of $C(52,5) = 2{,}598{,}960$. $P(\text{at least one ace}) = 1 - \frac{C(48,5)}{C(52,5)} = 1 - \frac{1712304}{2598960} \approx 0.341$. About one hand in three carries an ace.

commonMistakes.text: Complementing only part of the description.
commonMistakes.correction: The complement of "at least one six" is "no sixes" — not "at least one non-six". Write the complement in words and verify the two descriptions cover everything exactly once.

commonMistakes.text: Subtracting from the wrong side: P(A) − 1.
commonMistakes.correction: It's 1 − P(A). A negative probability is the formula upside down.

tryIt.statement: Three coins are flipped. Find $P(\text{at least one tail})$.
One die is rolled. Events: $A = $ "roll is even", so what is $P(\text{not } A)$ — and which outcomes form it?

steps.title: Events are clubs
beats: An event isn't one outcome — it's a SET of outcomes.
"Sum is even": 18 members out of 36 rolls.
$P(\text{event}) = \frac{\text{members}}{\text{universe}}$.
Every set idea you own now applies to chance.

steps.title: The complement, at probability scale
beats: $A$ and not-$A$ split the universe — no overlap, nothing missed.
$P(\text{not } A) = 1 - P(A)$.
Unit 1's subtraction, divided by the universe size.
Trigger: "at least one" → count "none" instead.

steps.title: Words first
steps.prompt: The complement of "at least one head in five flips" is…
steps.explanation: "At least one head" fails only when NO head appears — all tails, probability $\frac{1}{32}$. "At least one tail" overlaps the original event; "exactly one head" is inside it.

steps.title: Sum to one
steps.prompt: $P(\text{rain}) = 0.3$, and a forecaster claims $P(\text{no rain}) = 0.6$. Verdict?
steps.explanation: Rain and no-rain split the universe: $0.3 + 0.6 = 0.9 \ne 1$. The missing $0.1$ isn't weather, it's a bookkeeping hole. (Snow IS "no rain" — the complement absorbs everything else.)

steps.title: Hear 'at least', think '1 minus none'
steps.body: This reflex will fire constantly for the rest of the course — binomial distributions, card hands, quality control. Train it now: every "at least one" gets rewritten as 1 − P(none) BEFORE any counting starts. The direct route exists, and it's almost always case soup.

steps.title: The birthday paradox, properly yours now
steps.body: In a room of 23 people, P(some two share a birthday) ≈ 0.507 — computed exactly as this lesson taught: the complement (all 23 birthdays different) is one long shrinking-slot product, $\frac{365 \times 364 \times \cdots \times 343}{365^{23}}$, and 1 minus it wins bar bets. Every tool in that formula is now yours.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Events are subsets; probability is membership over universe.
P(not A) = 1 − P(A) — the piles must sum to 1 (free audit).
'At least one' → 1 − P(none), a reflex worth training.

lessons.title: The Addition Rule
lessons.concreteComparison: Draw one card: $P(\text{king}) = \frac{4}{52}$, $P(\text{heart}) = \frac{13}{52}$. So $P(\text{king or heart}) = \frac{17}{52}$? Count the club: $13$ hearts plus the $3$ non-heart kings $= 16$ — the king of hearts was standing in BOTH lines and got counted twice. $\frac{16}{52}$, not $\frac{17}{52}$. Unit 1's inclusion-exclusion, now with probabilities.
lessons.objective: Compute P(A or B) with the addition rule, recognizing when the overlap term vanishes (mutually exclusive events).
concept: **The addition rule:** $$P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B)$$ — inclusion-exclusion divided through by the universe size. The overlap was added twice; subtract it once.
**Mutually exclusive** events can't co-occur — the overlap is empty, $P(A \text{ and } B) = 0$ — and only then does plain addition hold: $P(\text{roll } 1 \text{ or } 2) = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6}$.
The rule runs in every direction: given any three of the four quantities $P(A), P(B), P(A \text{ and } B), P(A \text{ or } B)$, solve for the fourth like any equation.
Combined with complements it answers "neither": $P(\text{neither } A \text{ nor } B) = 1 - P(A \text{ or } B)$ — the outside of the union.
lessons.keyIdea: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B); the overlap term is 0 exactly when the events are mutually exclusive.

facts.title: The addition rule
facts.latex: P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)
facts.explanation: Inclusion-exclusion over the universe size — subtract the double-counted overlap.

facts.title: Mutually exclusive
facts.latex: P(A \cap B) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)
facts.explanation: No shared outcomes: plain addition is legal only here.

workedExamples.statement: One card is drawn. Find $P(\text{king or heart})$.
One die is rolled. Find $P(\text{even or greater than } 4)$.
workedExamples.solution: Even: $\{2,4,6\}$, $\frac{3}{6}$. Greater than 4: $\{5,6\}$, $\frac{2}{6}$. Overlap: $\{6\}$, $\frac{1}{6}$. Union: $\frac{3 + 2 - 1}{6} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}$ — the club $\{2, 4, 5, 6\}$, counted directly, agrees.

workedExamples.statement: In a class, $P(\text{plays music}) = 0.45$, $P(\text{plays sport}) = 0.60$, and $P(\text{plays both}) = 0.25$. Find the probability a random student plays NEITHER.
workedExamples.solution: Union first: $0.45 + 0.60 - 0.25 = 0.80$. Neither is the outside: $1 - 0.80 = 0.20$.

commonMistakes.correction: P(king) + P(heart) = 17/52 double-counts the king of hearts. Ask first: CAN both happen at once? If yes, the overlap term is mandatory.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming events with probabilities summing over 1 are impossible.
commonMistakes.correction: P(music) + P(sport) = 1.05 is fine — it just proves they overlap by at least 0.05. Only the UNION is capped at 1.

tryIt.solution: Odd $\frac{3}{6}$, six $\frac{1}{6}$, no overlap (6 is even): $\frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}$.

steps.title: The king of hearts, counted twice
beats: $P(\text{king}) = \frac{4}{52}$, $P(\text{heart}) = \frac{13}{52}$.
Add: $\frac{17}{52}$? Count the club: $13$ hearts $+ 3$ other kings $= 16$.
One card stood in both lines.
$P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(\text{both})$. Old friend, new outfit.

steps.title: Mutually exclusive: when adding is legal
beats: Some pairs CAN'T co-occur: roll a 1 or a 2. Overlap empty.
$P(\text{both}) = 0$ → plain addition holds.
That's the ONLY time it holds.
Ask "can both happen?" before every addition. Every time.

steps.title: Can both happen?
steps.prompt: Which pair of one-die events is mutually exclusive?
steps.explanation: No roll is both ≤2 and ≥5. But 6 is even AND a multiple of 3; and 3, 5, 7... — 3 and 5 are odd primes. Overlap radar beats intuition: check for a shared member.

steps.title: The rule as an equation
steps.explanation: $0.6 + 0.5 - 0.3 = 0.8$. The $1.1$ forgot the overlap (and broke the scale — a free red flag); $0.4$ subtracted twice.

steps.title: 'Or' is inclusive in mathematics
steps.body: In everyday speech "soup or salad" means one, not both. In probability, "A or B" ALWAYS includes the both-case — the union. If a problem means "exactly one of A, B", it must say so (and then you want P(A) + P(B) − 2P(A∩B)). Read "or" as "at least one of".

steps.title: Insurance is the addition rule at scale
steps.body: Actuaries price bundled policies with exactly this rule: P(claim on flood OR fire) can't be priced by adding the two rates when one storm causes both — the overlap is where bundled-policy discounts (and insurer bankruptcies) come from. The subtraction term has a dollar sign on it.

steps.title: What you learned
points: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B), always.
The overlap term is 0 only for mutually exclusive events — check, don't assume.
Neither = 1 − union; and the rule solves for any missing piece.

lessons.title: Two-Way Tables & Venn Diagrams
lessons.concreteComparison: A survey of $100$ students: $60$ have a bike, $45$ have a skateboard, $25$ have both. Quick — how many have a bike but NO skateboard? A Venn diagram answers before you finish reading: the bike circle holds $60$, its overlap holds $25$, so bike-only is $35$. Organize first, and every probability in the story becomes a lookup instead of a derivation.
lessons.objective: Organize joint information in Venn diagrams and two-way tables, then read off any probability — unions, overlaps, 'only's, and neithers.
concept: A **Venn diagram** stores four region counts (A-only, both, B-only, neither) that always sum to the total. Fill the OVERLAP first, then subtract to get the 'only' regions, then the outside absorbs the rest.

A **two-way table** stores the same information with room to grow: rows for A/not-A, columns for B/not-B — four cells plus margins. The margins (row and column totals) are the single-event counts; the cells are the joint counts.

Every probability in the story is now a lookup: $P(\text{bike only}) = \frac{35}{100}$, $P(\text{neither}) = \frac{20}{100}$, $P(\text{bike or skateboard}) = \frac{80}{100}$ — no formula juggling, just region arithmetic.

The two tools are one tool: the table's four inner cells ARE the Venn's four regions. Tables scale better to real data (and to conditional probability, next unit); Venns are faster for two-set word problems.
lessons.keyIdea: Fill the overlap first; the four regions sum to the total, and every probability becomes a region lookup.

facts.title: Region arithmetic
facts.explanation: Overlap first, subtract for the 'only' regions, the outside absorbs the rest.

facts.title: Table = Venn
facts.explanation: Two representations of the same joint counts; margins hold the single-event totals.

workedExamples.statement: Of $100$ students: $60$ have a bike, $45$ a skateboard, $25$ both. Build the Venn regions and find $P(\text{neither})$.
workedExamples.solution: Overlap: $25$. Bike-only: $60 - 25 = 35$. Skateboard-only: $45 - 25 = 20$. Sum so far: $80$; neither: $100 - 80 = 20$. $P(\text{neither}) = \frac{20}{100} = \frac{1}{5}$.

workedExamples.statement: From the same survey, find $P(\text{exactly one of the two})$.
workedExamples.solution: "Exactly one" is the two 'only' regions: $35 + 20 = 55$. $P = \frac{55}{100} = \frac{11}{20}$. (Note it's NOT the union $80$ — the union includes the both-region.)

workedExamples.statement: A school of $200$: $110$ study English, $80$ study Japanese, $150$ study at least one. Fill the table and find how many study BOTH.
workedExamples.solution: Addition rule backwards: $|E \cap J| = 110 + 80 - 150 = 40$. Regions: English-only $70$, both $40$, Japanese-only $40$, neither $200 - 150 = 50$. Cross-check: $70+40+40+50 = 200$ ✓.

commonMistakes.text: Writing the full set size into the 'only' region.
commonMistakes.correction: The bike circle's 60 includes the 25 both-owners. Bike-ONLY is 60 − 25 = 35. Fill the overlap first and this mistake becomes impossible.
'Or' (union) includes the both-region; 'exactly one' excludes it. On the Venn they're different region sets: 35+25+20 = 80 vs 35+20 = 55.

tryIt.statement: Of $50$ diners: $30$ ordered soup, $22$ ordered salad, $12$ ordered both. How many ordered neither?
From that table, find $P(\text{salad but no soup})$.

steps.title: Four regions, total known
beats: Two overlapping events split the world into FOUR regions.
A-only · both · B-only · neither — summing to the total.
Fill the OVERLAP first. Subtract for the 'only's. The outside absorbs the rest.
Then every question is a lookup.

steps.title: Read the regions
steps.prompt: With bike-only $35$, both $25$, skateboard-only $20$, neither $20$: $P(\text{has a skateboard})$?
steps.explanation: The skateboard circle is both + skateboard-only $= 25 + 20 = 45$. The $20$ is only-skateboard (forgot the overlap); $70$ grabbed a margin from the wrong event.

steps.title: The two-way table
beats: Rows: A / not-A. Columns: B / not-B. Four cells + margins.
Cells are joints; margins are singles; corner is the total.
It's the Venn, straightened — and it scales to real data.
Next unit's conditional probability reads straight off it.

steps.title: Fill the missing cell
steps.prompt: A table shows: total $80$, likes-cats $50$, likes-dogs $46$, likes-neither $4$. How many like BOTH?
steps.explanation: At least one: $80 - 4 = 76$. Both: $50 + 46 - 76 = 20$. The addition rule runs backwards through a table as easily as forward.

steps.title: Draw before you derive
steps.body: Any problem naming two overlapping categories earns a 20-second Venn or table BEFORE any probability formula. Regions make 'only', 'exactly one', 'neither', and 'or' unambiguous — most addition-rule errors are really region-reading errors that a drawing would have blocked.

steps.title: Venn's diagram was a logic machine
steps.body: John Venn introduced his diagrams in 1880 to mechanize syllogisms — Victorian logicians literally shaded regions to check arguments. Statisticians hijacked them for probability within a generation. The four-region picture you just drew is a 145-year-old proof assistant that still fits on a napkin.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Overlap first, 'only's by subtraction, outside absorbs the rest.
Tables = Venns with margins; both make probabilities lookups.
'Or', 'exactly one', 'neither' are region sets — draw, then read.

lessons.title: Probability with Chooses
lessons.concreteComparison: A quality inspector draws $3$ phones from a box of $12$ that hides $4$ defective units. $P(\text{all three fine})$? Universe: $C(12,3) = 220$ draws. Favorable: all from the $8$ good ones, $C(8,3) = 56$. $P = \frac{56}{220} = \frac{14}{55} \approx 0.25$. Act One's chooses now live inside probability fractions — numerator AND denominator.
lessons.objective: Compute probabilities of unordered draws using combinations in both layers of the fraction, including quota and at-least events.
concept: When a handful is drawn at once (cards, committee members, sampled products), the natural universe is UNORDERED: $C(n, r)$ draws, all equally likely.
Quota events put a choose per pool in the numerator (Unit 3): $P(\text{exactly } 2 \text{ defective of } 3) = \frac{C(4,2) \, C(8,1)}{C(12,3)}$ — defectives from defectives, good from good.
"At least one defective" bows to the complement reflex: $1 - P(\text{none}) = 1 - \frac{C(8,3)}{C(12,3)}$.

Sanity net: the exact-count probabilities over all possible counts must sum to $1$ — $P(0) + P(1) + P(2) + P(3) = 1$ for three draws. When your quota cases don't sum to one, a pool or a case is mislabeled. (This distribution-over-counts view becomes the star of Unit 7.)
lessons.keyIdea: Unordered draws: chooses upstairs and down — quota products for exact counts, complement for at-least, and exact-counts sum to 1.

facts.title: Quota probability
facts.explanation: Each category chosen from its own pool, over the unordered universe.

facts.title: The distribution audit
facts.explanation: Exact-count probabilities across all k must exhaust the universe.

workedExamples.statement: A box holds $8$ good and $4$ defective phones. Three are drawn at random. Find $P(\text{all good})$.
Same box, same draw. Find $P(\text{exactly one defective})$.
A $5$-card hand is dealt. Find $P(\text{all five are the same suit — a flush, counting straight flushes too})$.
workedExamples.solution: Choose the suit ($4$ ways), then $5$ of its $13$ cards: $4 \times C(13,5) = 4 \times 1287 = 5148$ hands. $P = \frac{5148}{2598960} = \frac{33}{16660} \approx 0.00198$ — about one hand in $505$.

commonMistakes.text: Mixing an unordered numerator with an ordered denominator.
commonMistakes.correction: If the universe is C(12,3) unordered draws, favorable must be unordered too. Mixing C's with slot-products silently changes one layer's universe — the fraction stops meaning anything.

commonMistakes.text: Computing 'at least one defective' by locking one defective in.
commonMistakes.correction: C(4,1)·C(11,2)/C(12,3) overcounts multi-defective draws (Unit 3's reserve-one trap, at probability scale). Complement: 1 − C(8,3)/C(12,3).

tryIt.statement: From $6$ red and $4$ blue marbles, $2$ are drawn together. Find $P(\text{both red})$.
Same bag: find $P(\text{at least one blue})$.

steps.title: Chooses move into the fraction
beats: Draw $3$ phones from $12$ ($4$ hidden defects).
Universe: $C(12,3) = 220$ unordered draws — equally likely.
All-good: $C(8,3) = 56$ of them.
$P = \frac{56}{220} \approx 0.25$. Act One upstairs AND down.

steps.title: Two defective
steps.prompt: Same box ($8$ good, $4$ bad, draw $3$): $P(\text{exactly two defective})$?
steps.explanation: Two bad from $4$, one good from $8$: $6 \times 8 = 48$ over $220$. Forgetting the good phone's choose ($6/220$) or drawing it from a mislabeled pool of $10$ both break the quota.

steps.title: Exact counts must sum to 1
beats: The universe is exhausted — nothing double-counted, nothing missed.
This little table is called a DISTRIBUTION. Unit 7 makes it the star.

steps.title: At least one, again
steps.prompt: Box of $8$ good, $4$ bad, draw $3$: best route to $P(\text{at least one defective})$?
steps.explanation: Complement of all-good: $1 - \frac{56}{220} = \frac{164}{220}$. Locking one defective in overcounts (the reserve-one trap); summing $P(1) + P(2)$ forgets $P(3)$.

steps.title: Write the fraction as a story
steps.body: Lay out every combinatorial probability as (quota chooses)/(universe choose) with the pools labeled: bad 4 | good 8 | draw 3. When the story is visible in the fraction, wrong pools and missing chooses announce themselves.

steps.title: Acceptance sampling ships your phone
steps.body: Factories accept or reject entire shipments based on exactly the box-of-phones computation: draw a sample, and if it holds too many defectives, the lot fails. International standards (ISO 2859) are printed tables of these choose-fractions — a warehouse gate is a hypergeometric probability with a barcode scanner.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Unordered draws: C(n,r) universe, quota chooses upstairs.
Exact-count probabilities sum to 1 — the distribution audit.

lessons.title: Odds & the Long Run
lessons.concreteComparison: A horse is quoted at odds of "$3$ to $1$ against". That's NOT a probability of $\frac{1}{3}$: it says for every $1$ way to win there are $3$ ways to lose — $P(\text{win}) = \frac{1}{4}$. And what does $\frac{1}{4}$ MEAN? Not "wins every fourth race" — it means that over MANY races, the winning fraction settles near $25\%$. Two translations — odds to probability, and probability to long-run frequency — finish this unit's foundation.
lessons.objective: Convert between odds and probability, and interpret probability as long-run relative frequency with expected counts.
concept: **Odds** compare favorable to UNfavorable: odds of $a$ to $b$ in favor means $P = \frac{a}{a+b}$. Odds $3$:$1$ against winning means $1$ favorable, $3$ unfavorable: $P(\text{win}) = \frac{1}{4}$. Probability compares favorable to TOTAL — the denominators differ.

**Long-run meaning:** $P = \frac{1}{4}$ promises nothing about four races. It says the observed fraction of wins CONVERGES toward $\frac{1}{4}$ as the number of races grows — the law of large numbers, informally.

**Expected count** scales the promise: in $n$ trials, expect about $n \times P$ occurrences — $200$ rolls of a die → about $\frac{200}{6} \approx 33$ sixes. "About" is honest: the count wobbles around the expectation, and the wobble SHRINKS relative to $n$.

The gambler's fallacy dies here: a coin that just landed heads five times has NO memory — $P(\text{heads})$ is still $\frac{1}{2}$. The long run balances by DILUTION (millions of future flips swamp five), not by compensation.
lessons.keyIdea: Odds a:b ↔ P = a/(a+b); probability is a long-run frequency — expected count ≈ nP, with no memory and no due-ness.

facts.title: Odds to probability
facts.explanation: Odds compare favorable to unfavorable; probability compares favorable to total.

facts.title: Expected count
facts.explanation: The long-run promise, scaled to n trials — a center, not a guarantee.

workedExamples.statement: A horse's odds are $3$:$1$ against. Find the implied probability of winning — and of losing.
workedExamples.solution: Against means $3$ unfavorable per $1$ favorable. $P(\text{win}) = \frac{1}{1+3} = \frac{1}{4}$; $P(\text{lose}) = \frac{3}{4}$. (Check: they sum to $1$ ✓.)

workedExamples.statement: One die: express $P(\text{roll a } 6)$ as odds in favor.
workedExamples.solution: Favorable: $1$ face; unfavorable: $5$ faces. Odds in favor: $1$:$5$. (Probability $\frac{1}{6}$ — note the different denominators: odds use $5$, probability uses $6$.)

workedExamples.statement: A basketball player makes $70\%$ of her free throws. In a season of $250$ attempts, how many makes should the team expect — and should anyone panic if she hits $165$?
workedExamples.solution: Expected: $250 \times 0.7 = 175$ makes. An observed $165$ is $10$ under — a wobble of $4\%$ of the attempts, well within normal variation for a season. Expected counts are centers, not contracts.

commonMistakes.text: Reading odds a:b as probability a/b.
commonMistakes.correction: Odds 3:1 against → P(win) = 1/4, not 1/3. Odds compare win-ways to lose-ways; probability needs the TOTAL underneath: a/(a+b).
Five heads in a row changes nothing: the next flip is still 1/2. Randomness has no memory; the long run balances by swamping the streak, not by reversing it.

tryIt.statement: Odds are $2$:$3$ in favor of rain. Find $P(\text{rain})$.
A spinner lands on gold with $P = \frac{3}{20}$. About how many golds in $400$ spins?

steps.title: Odds are not probabilities
beats: Odds $3$:$1$ against: $3$ lose-ways per $1$ win-way.
Odds compare favorable to UNfavorable.
Probability compares favorable to TOTAL. Add before dividing.

steps.title: Both directions
steps.explanation: Favorable $2$, total $7$ → unfavorable $5$: odds $2$:$5$ in favor. Writing $2$:$7$ mixes the two systems; $5$:$2$ states the odds AGAINST.

steps.title: What probability promises
beats: $P = \frac{1}{4}$ does NOT mean "every fourth time".
It means: over many trials, the fraction settles near $\frac{1}{4}$.
Expected count in $n$ trials: about $nP$ — a center with wobble.
The law of large numbers: more trials, tighter fraction.

steps.title: No memory
steps.prompt: A fair coin lands heads $5$ times running. $P(\text{heads on flip } 6)$?
steps.explanation: The coin stores nothing. Both "due" and "hot" project memory onto metal. The long run balances streaks by dilution — millions of future flips — not by compensation on the next one.

steps.title: Sportsbook odds contain a fee
steps.body: Real betting odds imply probabilities that sum to MORE than 1 across all outcomes — the excess is the bookmaker's margin. Convert quoted odds to probabilities and add them up: the total minus 1 is the house's cut. A useful literacy test for any market you meet.

steps.title: Kerrich's prison-camp experiment
steps.body: Interned in Denmark during WWII, mathematician John Kerrich flipped a coin 10,000 times and logged every result: 5,067 heads — 50.67%. His hand-made dataset remains the classic demonstration that observed frequency really does settle onto probability. The long run isn't a metaphor; a patient man in a camp measured it.

steps.title: What you learned — Unit 5 complete
points: Odds a:b ↔ P = a/(a+b): favorable-to-unfavorable vs favorable-to-total.
Probability is long-run frequency; expected count ≈ nP, wobble included.
No memory, nothing is 'due' — next: how INFORMATION changes chance.

practice.statement: A fair die is rolled once. Find $P(\text{prime number})$.
practice.solution: Primes on a die: $\{2, 3, 5\}$ — three of six: $P = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$.

practice.statement: Two dice are rolled. Find $P(\text{sum} = 7 \text{ or sum} = 11)$.
practice.solution: Sum 7: $6$ ways; sum 11: $2$ ways; mutually exclusive (one sum per roll): $\frac{6 + 2}{36} = \frac{8}{36} = \frac{2}{9}$.

practice.statement: Four coins are flipped. Find $P(\text{at least one head})$.
One card is drawn. Find $P(\text{queen or spade})$.
Of $60$ gym members, $35$ lift weights, $28$ swim, $15$ do both. Find $P(\text{a random member does neither})$.
A drawer holds $5$ black and $3$ white socks. Two are drawn together. Find $P(\text{both black})$.
From a shelf of $7$ novels and $3$ comics, $3$ books are grabbed at random. Find $P(\text{exactly one comic})$.
Odds are $4$:$1$ against a team winning. Find $P(\text{win})$, and the expected wins in a $40$-game season at that rate.

testYourself.statement: A $3$-digit code is chosen at random ($000$–$999$). Find $P(\text{all three digits are different})$.
Two dice: find $P(\text{sum is even or sum} > 9)$.
In a survey of $120$ students, $70$ use the library, $50$ use the gym, and $30$ use both. Find $P(\text{exactly one of the two})$ for a random student.
A jar holds $6$ mint, $5$ toffee, and $4$ chocolate candies. Three are drawn together. Find $P(\text{one of each type})$.
A $4$-person team is drawn at random from $5$ girls and $4$ boys. Find $P(\text{at least one boy})$.

A weather service says odds are $7$:$3$ in favor of snow. Convert to a probability, state the expected snowy days over the next $30$ days at that rate, and explain what the expectation does NOT promise.
testYourself.solution: $P = \frac{7}{10}$. Expected: $30 \times 0.7 = 21$ snowy days. It does NOT promise exactly $21$ — the observed count wobbles around the expectation; only the long-run FRACTION is pinned down.
